coredata.dc:title: Synergistic antibacterial effects of carbon dots derived from Lactobacillus acidophilus alone and in combination against carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Infection and Public Health
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of King Saud Bin Abdulaziz University for Health Sciences.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of King Saud Bin Abdulaziz University for Health Sciences.
coredata.dc:creator: Shamkhali, Leila
Mobarez, Ashraf Mohabati
Siadat, Seyed Davar
Pajavand, Hamid
coredata.dc:description:
Background Non-antibiotic treatments, such as carbon dots (C-dots), are gaining popularity in the search for effective alternatives. We aimed to evaluate the antibacterial properties of C-dots derived from Lactobacillus acidophilus (L-C-dots) alone and in combination against carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae (CRKP) isolates.
Methods Seventy clinical isolates of Klebsiella pneumoniae were tested for carbapenem resistance using a modified carbapenem inactivation assay. To determine the presence of fimH and mrkD genes, which are associated with biofilm formation, polymerase chain reaction (PCR) was conducted. L-C-dots were synthesized using a hydrothermal method and characterized. Furthermore, their antibacterial and anti-biofilm activities were evaluated against CRKP isolates. The combination of L-C-dots with the meropenem antibiotics were also tested using a checkerboard assay. Finally, the influence of L-C-dots on the expression levels of fimH and mrkD genes was examined using quantitative PCR (qPCR).
Results L-C-dots demonstrated significant antibacterial activity against CRKP isolates, with a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 50 mg/mL and a sub-MIC of 25 mg/mL. L-C-dots effectively inhibited biofilm formation at MIC and sub-MIC concentrations (P ˂ 0.05). Additionally, the L-C-dots had synergistic and additive interactions with the meropenem antibiotics against CRKP isolates, reducing the MIC of both agents. Furthermore, the L-C-dots decreased the expression of the fimH (p< 0.029) and mrkD (p < 0.015) genes.
Conclusions The findings indicate that L-C-dots may serve as a promising new treatment option for CRKP infections. They show potential as a strong therapeutic choice, especially when used in combination with traditional antibiotics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antibacterial
Carbapenem resistance
Carbon dot
Klebsiella pneumoniae
Lactobacillus acidophilus

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Phenotypic detection of carbapenemase-producing K. pneumoniae
Biofilm formation by K. pneumoniae isolates with crystal violet (CV) staining
PCR for detection of fimH and mrkD genes in K. pneumoniae isolates
Preparation of cell-free supernatant (CFS) of L. acidophilus
Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of L-C-dot and L-CFS
Antibiofilm activity of L-C-dot and L-CFS
Evaluation of the combined effects of L-C-dots and meropenem antibiotic
The characteristics of strains enrolled in the experiment
Detection of mrkD and fimH genes
Antibacterial activity of L-C-dots and L-CFSs
Monitoring expression of fimH and mrkD genes

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of King Saud Bin Abdulaziz University for Health Sciences.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The electrophoretic patterns of amplified K. pneumoniae fimbria genes, A) fimH and B) mrkD genes, were analyzed on agarose gel. The negative control was labeled as "ctrl (-)", the positive control as "ctrl (+)" for "K. pneumoniae ATCC 9997", and the ladders were 50 bp and 100 bp.
Biofilm formation by CRKP isolates with crystal violet staining.

Results of various physicochemical and biological characterization of L-C-dots: A) Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra comparing the L-C-dot (green), source bacteria (red), and lyophilized Cell-Free Supernatant (blue), highlighting functional group changes. B) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis displaying the size distribution of L-C-dot and C) Zeta Potential measurement of L-C-dots, showing their surface charge. D) 3D Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of L-C-dots, depicting excitation (y-axis), emission (x-axis), intensity (z-axis). E) 3D PL-excitation-emission contour maps showcasing the transition of photoluminescence color with increasing absorption wavelength. F) MTT assay used results for the relative viabilities of HeLa cells exposed to varying concentrations of L-C-dot, indicating concentration – dependent cytotoxic effect.

Antibiofilm Activity: (A) and (B) show inhibitory effects of L-CFSs and L-C-dots on biofilm formation in micro-plates at MIC and Sub-MIC concentrations, 25 mg/mL, and 12.5 mg/mL for L-CFSs, and 50 mg/mL and 25 mg/mL for L-C-dots, respectively. The x-axis represents five clinical CRKP isolates (labeled 1–5), with moderate and strong biofilms- forming capabilities. (C) and (D) present the calculation of average and individual optical density (OD) values of L-CFSs and L-C-dots treatment groups with un-treatment groups. (E) and (F) illustrate the percentage inhibition of biofilm formation, based on average and individual data, for L-CSFs and L-C-dots.

A) A schematic representation of a checkerboard test microplate containing a CRKP isolate. B) The actual microplate set up for the checkerboard assay (isolate NO.5). The labels on the microplate are defined as follows: MIC-A = L-C-dot, MIC-B = Meropenem antibiotic, Ctrl (+) = Positive control representing the growth of CRKP in culture media, Nut = Nutrient broth alone, and Mem = Meropenem antibiotic alone. C) A graphical depiction of the impact of L-dots at its MIC level, alone and in combination with meropenem, and D) A graphical representation of the impact of the Meropenem, and its MIC level alone and in combination with L-C-dots, emphasizing the synergistic and additive effects of the combination therapy.

(A) Results of qPCR analysis showing the expression levels of fimH and mrkD genes in CRKP isolates after treatment with L-C-dots. The data are presented as means ± standard deviations (SDs), highlighting the effects L-C-dots on these, biofilm associated genes. (B) Effect of L-C-dots on the expression of fimH gene in CRKP isolates. The graph displays the relative expression levels of fimH, with statistically significant downregulation observed following treatment. (C) Effect of L-C-dots on the expression of mrkD gene in CRKP isolates. The graph displays the relative expression levels of mrkD, with no significant change following treatment with L-C -dots. X-axis (panels B and C): Numbers 1–5 represent five CRKP clinical isolates capable of biofilm formation, each possessing both fimH and mrkD genes.
Primers sequence, annealing temperature and product sizes for PCR.
Combined effect of synthesized L-C-dots and the antibiotic meropenem (Mem).

head.ce:title: Synergistic antibacterial effects of carbon dots derived from Lactobacillus acidophilus alone and in combination against carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bacteriology, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Bacteriology, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bacteriology, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran/Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mycobacteriology and Pulmonary Research Department, Microbiology Research Center, Pasteur Institute of Iran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Mycobacteriology and Pulmonary Research Department, Microbiology Research Center, Pasteur Institute of Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology, Faculty of Medicine, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microbiology, Faculty of Medicine, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microbiology, Faculty of Medicine, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Non-antibiotic treatments, such as carbon dots (C-dots), are gaining popularity in the search for effective alternatives. We aimed to evaluate the antibacterial properties of C-dots derived from Lactobacillus acidophilus (L-C-dots) alone and in combination against carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae (CRKP) isolates.

Seventy clinical isolates of Klebsiella pneumoniae were tested for carbapenem resistance using a modified carbapenem inactivation assay. To determine the presence of fimH and mrkD genes, which are associated with biofilm formation, polymerase chain reaction (PCR) was conducted. L-C-dots were synthesized using a hydrothermal method and characterized. Furthermore, their antibacterial and anti-biofilm activities were evaluated against CRKP isolates. The combination of L-C-dots with the meropenem antibiotics were also tested using a checkerboard assay. Finally, the influence of L-C-dots on the expression levels of fimH and mrkD genes was examined using quantitative PCR (qPCR).

L-C-dots demonstrated significant antibacterial activity against CRKP isolates, with a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 50 mg/mL and a sub-MIC of 25 mg/mL. L-C-dots effectively inhibited biofilm formation at MIC and sub-MIC concentrations (P ˂ 0.05). Additionally, the L-C-dots had synergistic and additive interactions with the meropenem antibiotics against CRKP isolates, reducing the MIC of both agents. Furthermore, the L-C-dots decreased the expression of the fimH (p< 0.029) and mrkD (p < 0.015) genes.

The findings indicate that L-C-dots may serve as a promising new treatment option for CRKP infections. They show potential as a strong therapeutic choice, especially when used in combination with traditional antibiotics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae (CRKP) is a fast-spreading multidrug-resistant (MDR) pathogen is responsible for a variety of infections, including pneumonia, bacteremia, urinary tract infections, and liver abscesses [1,2]. The rising prevalence of antibiotic resistance among Klebsiella pneumoniae (K. pneumoniae) strains has led to the global spread of this MDR pathogen, particularly in hospital settings [3,4]. These pathogenic bacteria have developed the ability to survive even when exposed to therapeutic amounts of beta-lactam antibiotics, including carbapenems. A significant factor contributing to the pervasive presence of CRKP is its capacity to form biofilms [5]. Increasing antibiotic resistance in biofilm isolates has raised the possibility that new strategies to prevent or delay biofilm formation are urgently needed [6]. Biofilms provide protection for microorganisms against the host's immune system, specifically by preventing opsonization by antibodies and phagocytosis [7]. Moreover, bacteria within biofilms exhibit a much higher resistance to antibacterial agents compared to those in free-living planktonic form. Consequently, current treatment options often prove futile when attempting to treat infections that have already formed biofilms [8,9]. The development of nanomedicine has led to the creation of nanoparticles that can be used as antibacterial agents for treating bacterial infections [10]. One specific type of nanoparticle, known as carbon dots (C-dots), has shown great promise due to their small size and exceptional effectiveness against drug-resistant infections [11]. C-dots can be synthesized using various carbon sources, such as vitamin C, antibiotics, carbon nano-powders, metal nanoparticles, and bacteria [12]. These sources have shown activity against a wide range of Gram-positive and Gram-negative pathogens because of their remarkable properties such as reactive oxygen species (ROS) and damage to cell structures [13,14]. The diverse antibacterial mechanisms of nanoparticles (NPs) such as C-dots and their potential multivalent effects ensure the successful and widespread application of these nano agents in combating bacterial infections. However, the clinical use of certain metal-containing antibacterial NPs may be limited due to the toxicity of their metal components and the potential for long-term retention in the body [15,16].

Probiotic bacteria promote health and help control infectious bacteria through various mechanisms, including the prevention of pathogen adhesion, generation of ROS, down-regulation of toxin production, nutrient competition, and the production of bacteriocins and biosurfactants [12,16,17]. These beneficial bacteria are primarily administered orally to support the development of a healthy intestinal microflora; however, their effectiveness is often compromised by the harsh low pH environment of the gastrointestinal tract, which can kill them before they reach their target site [17,18]. Given the beneficial effects of probiotics, some researchers have utilized them alongside nanotechnology to synthesize nanoparticles like C-dots. Including, C-dots derived from Lactobacillus plantarum were discovered to decrease the adhesion and subsequent growth of an Escherichia coli (E. coli) biofilms [19]. Furthermore, C-dots derived from Bifidobacterium breve (B-C-dots) were found to disrupt the biofilm matrix of both E. coli and Salmonella typhimurium. Consequently, this disruption led to a reduction in bacterial density within the biofilms [17].

In this study, we synthesized carbon dots (L-C-dots) from probiotic bacteria, Lactobacillus acidophilus (L. acidophilus), using a one-step hydrothermal method. We aimed to test the hypothesis that these C-dots would retain the beneficial antibacterial properties of their bacterial source. Specifically, we examined their influence on the expression of the fimH and mrkD genes, which are associated with type 1 and type 3 fimbriae and the formation of biofilm development, against CRKP isolates. Furthermore, we explored the combined antibacterial effects of L-C-dots and meropenem antibiotics.

Seventy clinical isolates of K. pneumoniae were obtained from the bacteriology laboratory archives (all samples were collected before from patients admitted to a hospital in Tehran and were obtained from various body sites, with all samples archived in the microbiology laboratory). These isolates were cultured on 5 % sheep blood agar plates at a temperature of 35 ± 2 °C under aerobic conditions for overnight [20].

Lactobacillus acidophilus was already purchased as a lyophilized powder from Tak-Gen Zist, Iran and was available in the microbiology laboratory. It was grown on De Man, Rogosa, and Sharpe (MRS) medium agar (MRS, QUELAB, Germany) plates at the same temperature for overnight. Bacterial identification was carried out using phenotypic and molecular tests, as described in previous study [20,21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phenotypic detection of carbapenemase-producing K. pneumoniae
ce:section.ce:para: Carbapenem resistance detection was carried out on K. pneumoniae isolates and K. pneumoniae ATCC 9997. This was done using the modified carbapenem method (mCIM) and the EDTA carbapenem inactivation method (eCIM), following the guidelines set by the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) [22]. These procedures and results have been described in previous study [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biofilm formation by K. pneumoniae isolates with crystal violet (CV) staining
ce:section.ce:para:
Biofilm formation was measured using the crystal violet method [23,24]. Clinical isolates of K. pneumoniae were grown in Mueller Hinton Broth (MHB) medium at 35 ± 2 °C for overnight. Subsequently, 20 μL of 0.5 McFarland turbid bacterial suspension was mixed with 180 μL of MHB medium containing 1 % glucose to achieve a 1:10 dilution. The diluted solution was transferred to 96 sterile polystyrene micro-plates and incubated at 35 ± 2 °C for overnight. After incubation, the micro-plates were washed three times with PBS buffer, and the attached biomass was fixed with 99 % methanol for 15 minutes. The micro-plates were stained with 0.5 % crystal violet. After washing with water, the wells were dried at room temperature. Subsequently, the bound dye was released by adding 200 μL of 95 % ethanol. Bioﬁlm formation capacity was determined by measuring the absorbance at 492 nm using a microplate reader (2100 FAX STA Reader ELISA). The reference strain Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) PAO1 was chosen as the positive control, while culture media alone served as the negative control, due to their ability to form dense, tightly bound biofilms. The micro-plate readings classified the adherence capability of each bacterial cell into four categories based on absorbance values:
-
OD ≤ ODc = none.
-
ODc < OD ≤ 2 ODc = weak.
-
2 ODc < OD ≤ 4 ODc = moderate.
-
4 ODc < OD = strong biofilm formation.
The cut-off absorbance value (ODc) was established as three standard deviations (3 SD) above the mean OD of the negative control. Biofilm experiments were conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PCR for detection of fimH and mrkD genes in K. pneumoniae isolates
ce:section.ce:para: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) was used to detect the presence of fimH and mrkD genes (associated with biofilm formation) in CRKP isolates. The DNA from these isolates was extracted using the boiling method [25]. The primer sequences for the studied genes and the expected sizes [26] of the PCR products are provided in Table 1. The PCR procedure involved mixing 10 μL of Taq 2 × Master Mix, 1 μL of each primer, 7 μL of sterile distilled water, and 100 ng of bacterial DNA to make a total volume of 20 μL. Amplification was performed over 30 cycles after heating at 95 °C for 60 s. The amplification process followed these conditions: 94 °C for 30 s, 58 °C for 30 s at the annealing temperature for each primer, and 72 °C for 30 s. This was followed by a final step of 72 °C for 10 min. The PCR products were then analyzed by electrophoresis on a 1 % agarose gel at 100 V for 60 minutes. The gel was stained with Gel Red and then photographed. It was analyzed using a gel documentation system (Bio-Rad, Singapore).

Carbon dots were derived from L. acidophilus using the previously described one-step hydrothermal method [20]. Briefly, L. acidophilus was cultured in MRS broth, then the cells were washed and centrifuged. The resulting cell sediment was suspended in 40 mL of demineralized water and transferred to a Teflon-lined steel autoclave. This suspension was heated to 200 °C overnight in an oven. After heating, the mixture was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22-micrometer filter. The synthesized L-C-dots were then dialyzed, freeze-dried, and stored at − 20 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cell-free supernatant (CFS) of L. acidophilus
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare the Cell-Free Supernatants (CFSs) of L. acidophilus (L-CFSs) [28,29], isolated colonies were inoculated into 15 mL of MRS broth and incubated at 35 ± 2 °C overnight. After incubation, the samples were centrifuged at 6000 × g for 15 min to separate the CFSs from the bacteria. L-CFSs were obtained by filtering through a 0.22 micrometer filter and sterilizing. The supernatant collected from the culture medium of L. acidophilus was then lyophilized and stored in the refrigerator until used. Prior to use, they were rehydrated with sterile deionized water.

The physicochemical characteristics of L-C-dots were investigated using various techniques [17,20,30]. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM FEI TECNAI F20, Spain), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Dynamic light scattering (DLS), zeta potential analysis, Photoluminescence spectroscopy and cell viability was assessed using MTT assay conducted on HeLa (Human cervical cancer) cells (NCBI C115) obtained from the Pasteur Institute of Iran's cell bank. FTIR was also used to compare the source bacteria and CFS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of L-C-dot and L-CFS
ce:section.ce:para:
The antibacterial activities of L-C-dots and L-CFSs against CRKP isolates were determined using as reported previously [20], using the micro-dilution method in 96-well plates, following the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) guidelines [19,21]. Brieﬂy, 50 μL of MHB medium was added to wells in columns 2–12 of 96-well round-bottom non-tissue culture plates. 100 μL of antibacterial agents (L-C-dots and L-CFSs) with a stock concentration of 200 mg/mL was introduced into the first well of column 1. A 1:2 serial dilutions were performed from column 1 to column 10. 50 μL of bacterial suspension was added to each well, achieving a final bacterial concentration of approximately 5 ×
10
5
CFU/mL. For each test, the positive control consisted of a bacterial suspension with culture media, while the negative control included antibacterial agents (L-C-dots and L-CFSs) with culture media. Plates were incubated at 35 ± 2 °C overnight. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was identified as the lowest concentration of antibacterial agent that after inhibited bacterial growth, as indicated by the absence of turbidity. The optical density (OD) at 620 nm measured using an ELISA reader (Thermo/LabSystems 352 Multiskan, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiofilm activity of L-C-dot and L-CFS
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-biofilm activities of L-C-dots and L-CFSs was assessed against five selected CRKP clinical isolates. These isolates were chosen based on their strong or moderate biofilms forming capacities, the presence of fimH and mrkD genes, and their carbapenem resistance. The evaluation utilized the 96-well microtiter plate method, with modification, as described previous studies [28,31]. Also, the positive control was bacterial suspension with culture medium (without antibacterial agents), the negative control was culture medium only (no bacteria or antibacterial agents). Wells containing bacterial suspensions were treated with L-C-dots or L-CFSs. Plates were incubated at 35 ± 2 °C for a predetermined period. After incubation the wells were washed to remove planktonic bacteria, fixed, and stained with crystal violate. The biofilm formation was quantified by measuring the OD at 492 nm using a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the combined effects of L-C-dots and meropenem antibiotic
ce:section.ce:para:
The micro-broth checkerboard technique was used to evaluate the effects of meropenem (Mem) in combination with L-C-dots on five selected CRKP isolates [32,33]. The fractional inhibitory concentration (FIC) index was calculated for each combination by determining the highest dilution of the two antibacterial agents (L-C-dots and Mem) that inhibited visible bacterial growth. To determine the FIC index, the highest dilution of the antibiotic combination that showed no visible growth. Synergy was defined as FIC index of 0.5 or less, additive was defined as FIC index of 0.5–1, indifference was defined as FIC index between 1 and 4, and antagonism as FIC index of 4.0 or more. The combination response was evaluated by calculation of the friction inhibitory concentration index (FICI) as Eq. (1) and (2).
(1)
FICI = FICA + FICB
(2)
FICI = MIC of
A in combination / MIC of A alone + MIC of B in combination / MIC B alone

The impact of L-C dots on the expression of fimbriae genes (fimH and mrkD), for biofilm formation in five selected CRKP isolates, was evaluated using qPCR. The gene expression levels were compared with those of wild-type strains. The qPCR was performed with specific primers listed in Table 1. RNA extraction from CRKP isolates treated with L-C-dots (Sub-MIC concentrations = 25 mg/mL) was performed using a Viragene Akam Company kit, following the manufacturer's protocol. The purity of extracted RNA was determined using a nanodrop spectrophotometer (BioTek Epoch, USA) at OD: 260/280. cDNA synthesis was performed with the extracted RNA using the recommended conditions of the kit. The cDNA synthesis reaction mix consisted of 8 μL cDNA Synthesis Master Mix, 2 μL Random Hexamers, 1 μg total RNA, and DEPC-Treated Water (Up to 20 µL). The mixture was processed in a thermal cycler (Eppendorf AG, 22331, Germany) with a temperature program that included incubation at 25 °C for 10 min, 47 °C for 60 min, and 85 °C for 5 min. The synthesized cDNA samples were then used in qPCR reactions. For the qPCR, a mixture of 10 μL Real-Time SYBR Green 2X Master Mix, 0.7 μL of each primer, 1–5 µL cDNA, and Nuclease-free H2O was prepared to achieve a total volume of 20 μL. The protocol started with an initial denaturation at 94 °C for 4 min, followed by 40 cycles of denaturation at 95 °C for 30 s, annealing at 58°C for 45 seconds, and extension at 72 °C for 45 s. The final step was a 5-minute extension at 72 °C in a Rotor-Gene thermocycler (QIAGEN Rotor-Gene Q MDx, USA). The 16SrRNA [27] reference gene for normalization. Primer sequences and expected PCR product sizes are listed in Table 1.

Statistical analysis was conducted using Microsoft Excel 2016 and GraphPad Prism (version 8.0, San Diego, CA, USA). The data were analyzed using One-way ANOVA and t-test to assess the differences between groups. A significant level of p < 0.05, was used to determine statistical significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characteristics of strains enrolled in the experiment
Detection of mrkD and fimH genes
ce:section.ce:para: In the present study, the genetic material responsible for type 1 and 3 fimbriae (fimH and mrkD) was identified in all 70 isolates. This finding is consistent with global research, which indicates that the prevalence of these genes ranges from 69 % to 100 % across various studies [34,35]. The ability of K. pneumonia to form biofilms contributes significantly to the persistence of chronic urinary tract infections. K. pneumonia utilizes type 1 and type 3 fimbriae to adhere to surfaces, promoting colonization and biofilm formation [36]. These results underscore the importance of fimbriae in K. pneumoniae infections, they facilitate biofilm development and bacterial adhesion. Types 1 and 3 fimbriae, along with the bacterial capsule play a critical role in colonization, particularly in urinary tract infections. Notably, Type 3 fimbriae are consistently associated with enhanced biofilm formation, whereas the role of type 1 fimbriae is more nuanced and depend on experimental conditions and the host specific niche [37]. Given their pivotal role in biofilm formation and pathogenesis, targeting bacterial fimbriae represents a promising strategy for combating K. pneumonia. Fig. 1 A and B illustrate the agarose gel electrophoresis results of PCR product, confirming the presence of fimH and mrkD genes.

Our findings in the CV staining analysis (in Fig. 2) showed that 73 % isolates were able to produce biofilms (17 % strong (12/70), 37 % medium (25/70), 20 % weak (14/70), and 27 % were an inability to form biofilms (19/70), which is consistent with recent studies in 2023 and 2024 [38]. Several studies provide evidence that 44–82 % of K. pneumonia isolates are capable of biofilm formation [36,39,40]. The variation observed between different studies could be attributed to differences in bacterial origin, including genetic characterization, isolation source, and environmental conditions. Biofilm enhance bacterial resistant to antibiotics and host immune responses, making them more challenging to eradicate. Both biofilm formation and carbapenemase-production contribute significantly to the elevated levels of antibiotic resistance in K. pneumoniae. Therefore, developing antimicrobial effective against biofilm associated infection is critical. Despite the presence of fimH and mrkD genes in all isolates (as mentioned above), biofilm formation was absent in some isolates, potentially due to chromosomal mutation affecting regulatory systems [31].

HR-TEM analysis revealed that L-C-dots have a spherical shape with an average diameter of 5.83 ± 2.035 nm. This size is ideal for facilitating the penetration of bacteria cell wall, are suggested in previous study [20]. FTIR spectroscopy was used to investigate functional groups and is indicative of biochemical components including proteins, lipids, and polysaccharides [17,20]. The spectra of C-dot, CFS and their bacterial source showed absorption peaks at 3464, 3499 and 3411 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibrations of v(OH/N–H), indicating hydrogen bonding and confirming the presence of hydrate, hydroxyl and ammonium groups. Notably, the Amide I bands at 1667 cm−1, due to CO stretching vibrations, confirmed the presence of proteins in the C-dot ( Fig. 3 A). The presence of carboxyl (hydroxyl and carbonyl) groups in the surface further confirms the successful synthesis of the C-dots [17,20,31]. FTIR results also indicated that the C-dots are hydrophilic, enhancing their stability and dispensability in water due to the abundance of functional groups on their surface [41]. These functional groups, especially oxygen rich one, contribute to antimicrobial activity of C-dots by facilitating their absorption and diffusion into bacterial cells and improving their dispersion in solution. One notable point is that the existence of these functional groups allows L-C-dots to dissolve properly in water. This information emphasizes the significance of functional groups in the antimicrobial activity of C-dots, specifically oxygen-rich groups enhance the absorption and diffusion of C-dots into bacterial cells, as well as their dispersion ability in solution. Also, the antioxidant activity of nanoparticles improves biocompatibility because of the direct correlation between higher oxygen content. The presence of carboxyl (hydroxyl and carbonyl) groups in the surface further confirms the successful synthesis of the C-dots [17,20,31]. The DLS analysis, which determines the size distribution of particles in suspension, revealed that the L-C-dots had a size of 89.37 nm and a negative zeta potential of − 36.1 mV, as shown in Fig. 3 B and C. This negative zeta potential emphasizes the role of surface charge in electrostatic interactions with microbes [42].

Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) was used to visualize 3D photoluminescence spectrum of the L-C-dot (Fig. 3 D and E). The diagram shows a transition in the photoluminescence color from pale blue to green and eventually to red as the absorption wavelength increases, with the emission wavelength shifting from 521 to 698 nm. This is accompanied by a decrease in energy intensity density within this range. This item indicates that the functionality of the material is directly proportional to its surface chemistry, which in turn affects the sensitivity and quantum yield in detection applications [31,43–45]. Previous studies have further detailed these findings [20]. To assess the cytotoxicity of L-C-dots, we evaluated the viability of HeLa cells exposed to varying concentrations of L-C-dots (ranging from 0.05 to 6 mg/mL). As shown in Fig. 3 F, cell viability significantly decreased when exposed to L-C-dots concentrations of 6 and 3 mg/mL, with viability dropping to less than 10 % after 24-hours of incubation. In contrast, at lower concentrations of 1 and 0.5 mg/mL, cell viability remained high, reaching 70 % and 99.8 % respectively. These results align with previous research, which indicating varying levels of cytotoxicity across different cell lines, likely influenced by the source, synthesis method, and cell line characteristics. There is a direct correlation between the higher oxygen content of C-dots and their ability to protect cells from oxidative stress, thereby maintaining cell viability. However, the low presence of epoxy functional groups (C-O-C) increases oxidation potential, resulting in greater toxicity [11,19,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of L-C-dots and L-CFSs
ce:section.ce:para:
The present study investigates the antibacterial effect of L-C-dots and L-CFSs on K. pneumoniae ATCC 9997 and a biofilm-forming clinical isolate of K. pneumoniae . The study revealed that L-C-dots at a concentration of 50 mg/mL and L-CFSs at a concentration of 25 mg/mL exhibited a substantial inhibitory effect on the growth of isolates, suggesting their potential as effective antibacterial therapies. Therefore, L-CFS showed superior antibacterial activity. This efficacy is likely due to the presence of potent antibacterial compounds in the probiotics, such as bacitracin, bio-surfactant, and hydrogen peroxide. Cell-Free-Supernatants, alternatively designated post-biotics, have been the subject of extensive research due to their recognized antibacterial properties. On the other hand, given the persistent challenge of drug resistance, the developments of alternative strategies are imperative. Several studies have demonstrated that C-dots have the capacity to function as an effective antibiotic, exhibiting inhibitory effects across a broad spectrum [47]. Song et al. demonstrated that C-dots extracted from cigarette smoke exhibited a broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity. They further elucidated that this antibacterial property could be attributed to the destruction of the double helix structure of DNA [48]. In research, Ye et al. synthesized C-dots using a hydrothermal method from p-phenylenediamine. These C-dots particles showed surface charge-dependent bactericidal properties against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli isolates [49]. The extant research suggests that C-dots generally exhibit a greater antibacterial effect against gram-positive bacteria compared to gram-negative bacteria [14] . This disparity arises due to the structural differences in bacterial cell walls. The antimicrobial efficacy of C-dots depends on inherent properties such as size, shape, and charge, as well as any modifications made to their surface. It is evident that the nature and surface charge of C-dots play a pivotal role in determining their interaction with bacterial cell membranes. This underscores the significance of their physiochemical properties in the development of effective antibacterial strategies [14,31]. The synthesized L-C-dots exhibited a negative surface charge, likely attributable to their bacterial source, which hindered effective electrostatic interactions with CRKP isolates [11,14,15,31]. In addition, C-dots exert their antimicrobial effects primarily through the generation of ROSs, which compromise bacterial cell structure, induce cytoplasmic leakage via DNA binding, and regulate bacterial gene expression [11,14,31]. In the study conducted by Wu it was reported that the C-dots derived from Bifidobacterium
breve had the capacity to reduce the volume of bacteria by producing a substantial amount of ROSs [17]. Recent studies have focused on carbonized compounds derived from probiotic bacteria as cost effective, straightforward solutions. These probiotic-derived C-dots can serve as alternatives to intact probiotics bacteria and have demonstrated promise as biofilm-disrupting agents without requiring additional surface modification [17,20,50].

In this study, both L-C-dots and L-CFSs exhibited significant efficacy in preventing biofilm formation at MIC and Sub-MIC levels against five selected CRKP isolates. In order to evaluate the efficacy of the treatments, the mean OD values of the three groups (i.e. the untreated control, the L-CFS-treated and the L-C-dot-treated groups) were calculated. Analysis showed a statistically significant difference in biofilm inhibition between the treated and control groups (L-CFSs P < 0.003 and L-C-dots P < 0.034).

The results of the antibiofilm activity are presented in Fig. 4 A and B as a 96-well micro-plate format, while Fig. 4 C and D display the results in graphical form. Additionally, Fig. 4 E and F depict the inhibition rates of biofilm formation for L-C-dots and L-CFSs. These figures highlight the superior biofilm inhibitory activity of L-CFSs compared to L-C-dots, underscoring their potential as effective agents against CRKP biofilms. The results suggest that both treatments could serve as promising tools in combating biofilm-related infections caused by drug–resistant bacteria. Although this study did not observe structural changes in biofilms and fimbriae using electron microscopy, we recognize that such analyses could offer valuable insights into the mechanisms of L-C-dot action. This represents an important direction for future research, and we aim to address this limitation in our upcoming studies.

The present study demonstrated that the combination of L-C-dots with meropenem was effective in the inhibition of the growth of five selected CRKP isolates, in comparison with treatments using either agent alone ( Fig. 5 A and B). Table 2 and Fig. 5 C and D further highlight the synergistic and additive effects of this combination therapy, showing a significant reduction in bacterial growth with mean reduction of 2.31 ± 2.19 (P < 0.0001). This finding is consistent with the conclusions of previous research, which suggested that the combination of nanoparticles, such as C-dots, with therapeutic agents enhances the stability, solubility, and permeability of the drug compared to using pure drug solutions alone. This enhanced efficacy has been attributed to the unique properties of C-dots, including their small size, functionalized surface, stability (physiological parameters and temperature changes), solubility, and biocompatibility (the necessity to ensure the safe application of C-dots in various biological environments). Numerous studies have explored the application of C-dots combination therapies, focusing on their antibacterial potential against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [17,31].

C-dots derived from probiotic bacteria exhibit significant as potential as an alternative live bacterium due to their robust antibacterial properties. When combined with existing antibiotics, these C-dots demonstrate synergistic and additive effects, effectively combating resistant infections. This approach not only enhances antibacterial efficacy, but also allows for substantial reduction in drug dosage, thereby minimizing associated cytotoxicity. Importantly, the functionalities of C-dots remain stable and unaffected by antibiotics in such combination therapies [17,31,51]. It is anticipated that forthcoming research will concentrate on in vivo studies with a view to further elucidating the therapeutic advantages of L-C-dots. It is evident that a pivotal domain of inquiry pertains to the exploration of the efficacy and prospective advantages inherent in the amalgamation of L-C-dots with antibiotics. This endeavor signifies the establishment of a novel therapeutic modality for the management of multidrug-resistant pathogens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring expression of fimH and mrkD genes
ce:section.ce:para: The present report identifies the effect of C-dots, which have been synthesized, on the expression of biofilm regulatory genes. The genes of interest are fimH and mrkD, which are found in K. pneumoniae. Fig. 6 A displays the average results from the samples, while Fig. 6 A and B present data for each individual isolate. It has been demonstrated that there was no statistically significant decrease in mrkD gene expression, with 1.02-fold (p < 0.015), and a statistically significant decrease of 0.852-fold in fimH gene expression following exposure to L-C-dots (p < 0.029). The genes fimH and mrkD are crucial for biofilm formation in K. pneumoniae, as they produce type 1 and type 3 fimbriae. Furthermore, geographic variation can play a role in the expression of genes effective in biofilm formation [31,34,35,52]. These variations suggest the need for localized studies to fully understand gene prevalence and regulatory mechanism [36]. Liang et al. demonstrated that carbon quantum dots, derived from tinidazole, could reduce the expression of fimA, rgp (A, B), and kgp genes, which have been identified as being effective in the formation of biofilms. Therefore, it can be concluded that this nanoparticle has the potential to affect the self-assembly of proteins and to inhibit the formation of biofilms[53]. In a separate study conducted in Iran, it was observed that graphene quantum dots (GQD-Cur) derived from curcumin exhibited a substantial augmentation in the production of ROSs, concomitant with a decline in the expression of rcpA, fimA, and inpA genes, with respective reductions of –1.8, –6.9, and 8.11 times [54]. Based on current finding, several key areas for future research include: a deeper investigation into the mechanisms governing suggest virulence genes, including fimH and mrkD, is needed. Identifying additional biofilm related genes and pathways will provide a broader understanding of biofilm formation and persistence in resistant pathogens.

The findings of this study demonstrate that L-C-dots, when employed in conjunction with the antibiotic meropenem, enhance the stability, solubility, biocompatibility, and permeability of the drug when compared with the drug administered in isolation. Moreover, the reduction in the requisite effective dosage of antibiotics engendered by C-dots has been demonstrated to result in a concomitant diminution of their dose-dependent toxic effects. This development introduces innovative nanoscale techniques for creating antibacterial therapies and opens new avenues for treating CRKP infections. Further research is necessary to explore the underlying mechanisms and assess the therapeutic potential of C-dots.

The research received approval from the Medical Ethics Committee of Tarbiat Modares University (Approval Code: IR.MODARES.REC.1401.048) prior to commencing the study. The experiments were performed on previously isolated bacteria from clinical specimens of hospitalized patients. The patients did not directly participate in this research work.

Carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae (CRKP); Carbon dots (C-dots); C-dots derived from Lactobacillus acidophilus (L-C-dots); C-dots derived from Bifidobacterium breve (B-C-dots); Cell-Free Supernatant (CFS); Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI); Crystal Violet (CV); cut-off absorbance value (ODc); De Man, Rogosa, and Sharpe (MRS); DLS (Dynamic light scattering); eCIM (EDTA carbapenem inactivation method); Escherichia coli (E. coli); Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR); fractional inhibitory concentration (FIC); friction inhibitory concentration index (FICI); High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM); HeLa (Human cervical cancer); Klebsiella pneumoniae (K. pneumoniae); Lactobacillus acidophilus (L. acidophilus); Meropenem (Mem); Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC); mCIM (modified carbapenem method); Mueller Hinton Broth (MHB); multidrug-resistant bacteria (MDR); Nanoparticles (NPs); negative control "ctrl (-)"; optical density (OD); Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL); polymerase chain reaction (PCR); positive control "ctrl (+)"; Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) PAO1; Quantitative PCR (qPCR); Reactive oxygen species (ROS); three standard deviations (3SD).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no conflict of interest to report.

One-step hydrothermal synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots with selective antibacterial activity against Porphyromonas gingivalis. Nanoscale [Internet]. 2017 Jun 1 [cited 2022 Jan 6];9(21):7135–7142. Available from: https://xlink.rsc.org/?DOI=C7NR02128C

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradable carbon dots from cigarette smoking with broad-spectrum antimicrobial activities against drug-resistant bacteria

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Song Y., Lu F., Li H., Wang H., Zhang M., Liu Y., et al. Degradable Carbon Dots from Cigarette Smoking with Broad-Spectrum Antimicrobial Activities against Drug-Resistant Bacteria. ACS Appl Bio Mater. 2018;1(6):1871–1879.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step and one-precursor hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots with superior antibacterial activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ye Z., Li G., Lei J., Liu M., Jin Y., Li B. One-Step and One-Precursor Hydrothermal Synthesis of Carbon Dots with Superior Antibacterial Activity. ACS Appl Bio Mater. 2020;3(10):7095–7102.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bacteria-derived carbon dots inhibit biofilm formation of Escherichia coli without affecting cell growth

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lin F., Li C., Chen Z. Bacteria-derived carbon dots inhibit biofilm formation of Escherichia coli without affecting cell growth. Front Microbiol. 2018;9(FEB):1–9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibacterial effects of carbon dots in combination with other antimicrobial reagents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dong X., Awak M. Al, Tomlinson N., Tang Y., Sun Y.P., Yang L. Antibacterial effects of carbon dots in combination with other antimicrobial reagents. PLoS One. 2017;12(9):1–16.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Curcumin carbon dots inhibit biofilm formation and expression of esp and gelE genes of Enterococcus faecium

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sadaqat M.H., Mobarez A.M., Nikkhah M. Curcumin carbon dots inhibit biofilm formation and expression of esp and gelE genes of Enterococcus faecium. Microb Pathog. 2022;173(December):1–9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Specific anti-biofilm activity of carbon quantum dots by destroying p. Gingivalis biofilm related genes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liang G., Shi H., Qi Y., Li J., Jing A., Liu Q., et al. Specific anti-biofilm activity of carbon quantum dots by destroying p. Gingivalis biofilm related genes. Int J Nanomedicine. 2020;15:5473–5489.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoexcitation triggering via semiconductor graphene quantum dots by photochemical doping with curcumin versus perio-pathogens mixed biofilms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Pourhajibagher M., Parker S., Chiniforush N., Bahador A. Photoexcitation triggering via semiconductor Graphene Quantum Dots by photochemical doping with Curcumin versus perio-pathogens mixed biofilms. Photodiagnosis Photodyn Ther. 2019;28:125–131.
